assistant_output_excerpt:
I’ll first inspect the workspace and available Ruby/Rails versions through `mise`, then I’ll generate or adapt the Rails app in place. The requested `edit`/`write` tools are not available in this runtime, so I’ll keep changes tool-based through the available shell/generator workflow and avoid exposing any secret values.
The workspace itself appears empty, while git has unrelated changes outside this project directory. Rails is not a mise-managed tool here, so I’m going to use the newest mise Ruby available and install/use Rails as the gem for that Ruby.
I verified the current RubyLLM/OpenRouter docs before wiring this: RubyLLM supports `config.openrouter_api_key`, OpenRouter lists `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6` as the current Sonnet model, and RubyLLM supports `RubyLLM.chat(...).ask`. Next I’m installing/generating the Rails skeleton in the empty project directory.
The project directory is empty, so I can generate the app directly in place. I’m using Rails 8.1.3 on Ruby 4.0.2, skipping the Rails frameworks the app does not need while keeping Tailwind, importmap, Turbo, and Stimulus.
Rails generated a couple of deployment extras by default. I’m trimming those out and then I’ll add the RubyLLM service/controller/view structure around the lean Rails app.
I’m going to replace the generator placeholders with a small set of PORO components: `ChatMessage`, `Conversation`, a RubyLLM service, a controller, helpers, partials, a Stimulus composer controller, and focused tests. The app will keep chat state in the Rails session since Active Record is intentionally absent.
The first boot check caught an initializer ordering issue: Rails does not autoload `app/services` early enough for the RubyLLM initializer. I’m moving that default model string directly into the initializer and cleaning a couple of generator leftovers.
The generated routes now boot, and the app has the intended root plus POST/DELETE chat endpoints. I’m moving into focused verification now: tests first, then style/security checks, fixing anything concrete that comes back.
One controller test failed because `minitest/mock` was not required, so the class stub helper was unavailable. I’m adding that test dependency and rerunning the checks.
Minitest 6 in this bundle no longer ships `minitest/mock`, so I’m switching the controller test to an explicit controller factory hook instead of stubbing class methods. That keeps the test clear and avoids adding a mocking dependency.
Tests are green now with 95% line coverage. I’m running the CI-quality checks next: RuboCop, Brakeman, and bundle-audit.
Security checks are clean and bundle-audit found no vulnerable gems. RuboCop only reported formatting issues, so I’m applying autocorrect and rerunning it.
I’m tightening the local Docker path now: compose should run the same `bin/dev` process as local development, bind Rails to `0.0.0.0`, and avoid carrying the generated Rails master key since this app uses environment variables instead of credentials.
Rails still boots without credentials and returns an empty credentials hash, which is what this app needs. I’m rerunning the full CI script after the Docker and Gemfile adjustments.
The full CI script is passing. I’m starting a local Rails server in the background now so the app is actually reachable; Tailwind has already been built, so the plain Rails server is enough for this phase.
The first server launch exited immediately without useful output, so I’m restarting it with the chosen port expanded explicitly and then checking the health endpoint again.
Implemented the Rails app in place.

Built a lean Rails 8.1.3 / Ruby 4.0.2 app with no Active Record, no mailer, no job framework, no Action Cable, and a session-backed RubyLLM chat UI. Main pieces are in [ChatsController](/mnt/data/Projects/llm-coding-benchmark/results/gpt_5_5_codex/project/app/controllers/chats_controller.rb), [RubyLlmChat](/mnt/data/Projects/llm-coding-benchmark/results/gpt_5_5_codex/project/app/services/ruby_llm_chat.rb)

model.label: GPT 5.5 xHigh (Codex)
model.selection_reason: GPT 5.5 via Codex CLI at xhigh reasoning effort. Successor to GPT 5.4 — matches gpt_5_4_codex config exactly so the comparison measures model capability delta, not harness differences. Expected to produce similar ~$16/run cost band.
